title: Live collaboration's only UI surface is the document toolbar History view, not per-editor cursors.
content:
## Decision

Live collaboration on a document exposes **no per-editor cursors, no collaborative selection highlights, and no per-field presence**. The only live surface is the document toolbar's existing **History view**, where peer ops appear in real time, attributed to the correct signer.

## Rationale

- Powerhouse editors are too varied — tables, visual canvases, dashboards, button grids, forms — for a single cursor/selection abstraction to be meaningful across all of them.
- Building per-editor collaborative cursor support would require each editor to opt in with custom instrumentation, a large ongoing maintenance cost.
- The History view is already part of every document editor, already renders op-by-op, and already attributes ops to signers. Adding a real-time feed into it is the minimal integration.

## UX implication

Collaboration feels like 'watching the history tick in real time' rather than 'watching someone type in the same document'. That's a deliberate tradeoff — the reactor's op-attributed model is richer than a cursor ever could be, just presented differently.

## What still needs design

- A **Collaborate tab** in the chat panel: pick drive/doc, multi-select participants, send invite → arrives as a chat card with `[Join]` / `[Decline]`.
- Drive-level ACT first, doc-level narrowing later.
description: Powerhouse editors vary too widely (tables, canvases, dashboards, button grids) for meaningful cursors. Peer ops appear in the History timeline, attributed to the signer.
